title: Florida Homeowners Insurance Rates Are Rising Again in 2025-2026 — Here's What You Need to Know
summary: Florida homeowners are facing another round of premium increases in 2025 and 2026. We break down why rates keep climbing and what you can do to keep your costs manageable.
body:
If your homeowners insurance renewal notice felt like a gut punch this year, you're not alone. Florida homeowners have seen average premiums climb 20–40% over the past three years, and 2025–2026 is bringing yet another wave of increases for many policyholders.

Why Are Rates Still Going Up?

Several factors are driving Florida's insurance crisis:

  • Reinsurance costs: The carriers that insure insurance companies — called reinsurers — drastically raised their rates after back-to-back hurricane seasons. Those costs get passed directly to consumers.
  • Claims fraud and litigation: Florida historically accounted for over 75% of all U.S. homeowners insurance lawsuits while representing just 9% of claims. Recent tort reform has helped, but the damage to carrier loss ratios is still being absorbed.
  • Inflation in building materials: The cost to rebuild a damaged home has risen sharply since 2020 due to supply chain disruptions and labor shortages. Higher replacement costs mean higher premiums.
  • Carrier insolvencies: More than a dozen Florida-focused carriers went insolvent since 2020, shrinking the market and reducing competition.

What Can You Do?

You have more options than you think. Working with an independent agent who represents 50+ carriers — like the team at HOInsurance.com — means we can shop your risk across the entire market to find the best available rate. We also review your current policy for coverage you may not need, or gaps you didn't know existed.

Don't just accept your renewal. Call us at 800-616-1418 before it renews and let us compare your options.

title: Citizens Insurance Is Pushing Policyholders Out — What the Depopulation Program Means for You
summary: Florida's state-backed Citizens Property Insurance has been aggressively removing policies through its depopulation program. Find out if you're affected and what your options are.
body:
Florida's Citizens Property Insurance Corporation was created as an insurer of last resort — a safety net for homeowners who couldn't find coverage in the private market. But with its policyholder count soaring past 1.3 million in recent years, Citizens has been on an aggressive mission to shrink its book of business through what's known as the depopulation program.

How Depopulation Works

Under the program, private insurers submit offers to Citizens policyholders. If a private carrier offers coverage at a rate within 20% of your Citizens premium, Citizens can automatically transition your policy — even if you don't actively choose to switch.

This means you could receive a letter saying your Citizens policy is being transferred to a private carrier you've never heard of. It's legal, and it's happening to tens of thousands of Floridians every quarter.

Is That a Bad Thing?

Not necessarily. Some of the carriers taking on depopulated policies are solid, A-rated companies. But others are newer, less capitalized carriers with unproven claims histories — and the replacement coverage may not be identical to your Citizens policy.

What You Should Do

If you receive a depopulation notice, don't just accept the replacement policy blindly. Read it carefully, compare the coverages, and check the new carrier's financial rating. Better yet, call an independent agent who can compare the offer against the full market.

The HOInsurance.com team has helped hundreds of Florida homeowners navigate depopulation letters. Call us at 800-616-1418 — we'll review your options at no cost.

title: Hurricane Season 2025: How Florida Homeowners Can Prepare — And Why Your Insurance Matters
summary: Hurricane season runs June 1 through November 30. Here's a practical checklist for Florida homeowners to prepare their homes and make sure their insurance coverage is solid before the next storm.
body:
Atlantic hurricane season officially runs June 1 through November 30, but storm activity can begin earlier. For Florida homeowners, preparation isn't optional — it's survival. Here's what you should be doing right now.

Home Preparation Checklist

  • Inspect your roof: Loose or missing shingles are your biggest vulnerability. A roof inspection before the season costs far less than a claims dispute after one.
  • Clear gutters and downspouts: Standing water from a clogged gutter can cause interior water damage that your wind policy won't cover.
  • Trim trees and branches: Overhanging limbs become projectiles in a tropical storm. Remove deadwood proactively.
  • Secure or store outdoor furniture: Patio furniture can cause severe damage to windows and neighboring property in high winds.
  • Install hurricane shutters or impact windows: This also qualifies you for significant premium discounts in Florida.

Insurance Preparation Checklist

  • Review your hurricane deductible: Florida policies often have a separate hurricane deductible — typically 2–5% of your home's insured value — that's separate from your standard deductible. On a $400,000 home, a 2% hurricane deductible means you pay the first $8,000 out of pocket.
  • Confirm your dwelling coverage limit: Make sure your policy covers the full replacement cost of your home, not just the market value. Building costs have risen dramatically.
  • Check your flood coverage: Standard homeowners policies DO NOT cover flood damage — not even storm surge from a hurricane. A separate flood policy is essential.
  • Document your belongings: Take a video walkthrough of every room before storm season. Store it in the cloud.

Not sure if your coverage is adequate? Call the HOInsurance.com team at 800-616-1418 for a free pre-season coverage review.

title: Private Flood Insurance vs. NFIP in Florida: Which One Is Right for Your Home?
summary: The National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) isn't your only option in Florida. Private flood insurance has grown significantly — and in many cases, it offers better coverage at a lower price.
body:
When most Florida homeowners think about flood insurance, they think about the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) — the federal government program administered through FEMA. But the private flood insurance market in Florida has exploded over the past several years, and for many homeowners, private coverage is both cheaper and better.

What Is the NFIP?

The NFIP has been the dominant flood insurer in the U.S. since 1968. Coverage is standardized, federally backed, and sold through private agents. Pros: it's widely accepted by mortgage lenders, and it's stable. Cons: coverage limits are capped ($250,000 for the structure, $100,000 for contents), rates are formula-driven and not always competitive, and there's a mandatory 30-day waiting period for new policies.

What's Different About Private Flood Insurance?

Private flood carriers underwrite risk independently using their own models. Key advantages:

  • Higher coverage limits: Some private carriers offer $1 million or more in dwelling coverage.
  • Replacement cost coverage: NFIP pays actual cash value (depreciated) for contents; many private carriers pay replacement cost.
  • Shorter waiting periods: Some private carriers have 14-day or even same-day waiting periods for certain situations.
  • Additional living expenses: NFIP doesn't cover your temporary housing costs if a flood forces you out. Private carriers often do.
  • Often lower premiums: Especially for homes in Flood Zone X (moderate to low risk), private carriers frequently beat NFIP rates significantly.

Which Is Right for You?

It depends on your flood zone, home value, mortgage requirements, and risk tolerance. A flood insurance specialist can run both quotes side-by-side so you can compare apples to apples.

The team at HOInsurance.com specializes in both private and NFIP flood insurance. Call us at 800-616-1418 or get a quote online — we'll show you exactly what each option costs and covers.

title: Your Florida Insurer Dropped You — Now What? A Step-by-Step Guide
summary: Getting a non-renewal notice from your Florida homeowners insurance carrier is stressful — but it's not the end of the road. Here's exactly what to do when your insurer decides not to renew your policy.
body:
Non-renewal notices have become an uncomfortable reality for Florida homeowners over the past several years. Major carriers have pulled back from coastal zip codes, older homes, and properties with recent claims history. If you've received a non-renewal notice, here's what you need to do — and fast.

Step 1: Don't Panic — But Don't Wait

A non-renewal notice gives you a deadline, typically 45–120 days before your policy expires. That's actually enough time to find a solid replacement — if you start immediately. Waiting until the week before expiration dramatically limits your options.

Step 2: Understand Why You Were Non-Renewed

Common reasons include:

  • Roof age (many carriers won't insure roofs over 15–20 years)
  • Previous claims history (especially water or wind claims)
  • Carrier withdrawing from certain Florida zip codes entirely
  • Home condition issues identified during an inspection
  • Credit or loss history flags

Understanding the reason helps your new agent position your risk accurately to replacement carriers.

Step 3: Contact an Independent Agent Immediately

An independent agent who represents many carriers can cast a wide net on your behalf. This is very different from a captive agent (like State Farm or Allstate agents) who can only offer their one company's products.

Step 4: Address Roof Issues If Possible

If your non-renewal is roof-related, getting a roof inspection (or roof replacement quote) can actually help you get coverage. Some carriers will insure an older roof if you can demonstrate it's in good condition, or if you agree to a roof escrow or surcharge.

Step 5: Consider Citizens as a Last Resort

If the private market truly has no options for you, Citizens Property Insurance Corporation exists as Florida's insurer of last resort. Coverage is available to any Florida homeowner who can't find private market coverage. However, Citizens rates can be high, and it should be considered a temporary solution while you work to improve your insurability.

The HOInsurance.com team specializes in hard-to-place Florida homes. Whether you have an older roof, a coastal location, or a recent claim, we know which carriers are most likely to work with your situation. Call us today at 800-616-1418 — don't let a non-renewal notice catch you without coverage.
